section.title: The Transfiguration and the Call to Follow
section.sectionRationale: On the mountain the Father unveils the glory of His chosen Son and commands, Listen to Him. Coming down, the disciples fail before a demon, quarrel about greatness, and cannot grasp the coming cross. Then, at verse 51, the Gospel turns on its hinge: Jesus resolutely sets His face for Jerusalem, and the long travel narrative begins. The section closes with three would-be followers and the single-minded cost of the kingdom.
section.thesis: The transfigured Son whom the Father tells us to heed is the same Son who steadfastly walks toward the cross at Jerusalem, and to follow Him is to take that road with undivided, unlooking-back devotion.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the glory on the mountain and the Father's voice; the boy no one else could heal; the passion foretold amid an argument over greatness; the great hinge as Jesus sets out for Jerusalem; and the cost of following Him.

units.label: The glory on the mountain
units.note: This is My Son; listen to Him

verses.text: About eight days after Jesus had said these things, He took with Him Peter, John, and James, and went up on a mountain to pray.
And as He was praying, the appearance of His face changed, and His clothes became radiantly white.
Suddenly two men, Moses and Elijah, began talking with Jesus.
They appeared in {{doxa|glory}} and spoke about His {{exodos|departure}}, which He was about to accomplish at Jerusalem.
Meanwhile Peter and his companions were overcome by sleep, but when they awoke, they saw Jesus’ glory and the two men standing with Him.

As Moses and Elijah were leaving, Peter said to Jesus, “Master, it is good for us to be here. Let us put up three shelters, one for You, one for Moses, and one for Elijah.” (He did not know what he was saying.)
While Peter was speaking, a cloud appeared and enveloped them, and they were afraid as they entered the cloud.
And a voice came from the cloud, saying, “This is My Son, whom I have {{eklegomai|chosen}}. Listen to Him!”
After the voice had spoken, only Jesus was present with them. The disciples kept this to themselves, and in those days they did not tell anyone what they had seen.

units.label: The boy no one else could heal
units.note: an unbelieving generation, and the greatness of God

verses.text: The next day, when they came down from the mountain, Jesus was met by a large crowd.
Suddenly a man in the crowd cried out, “Teacher, I beg You to look at my son, for he is my only child.
A spirit keeps seizing him, and he screams abruptly. It throws him into convulsions so that he foams at the mouth. It keeps mauling him and rarely departs from him.
I begged Your disciples to drive it out, but they were unable.”
“O unbelieving and perverse generation!” Jesus replied. “How long must I remain with you and put up with you? Bring your son here.”
Even while the boy was approaching, the demon slammed him to the ground in a convulsion. But Jesus rebuked the unclean spirit, healed the boy, and gave him back to his father.

units.label: The passion foretold, and the argument over greatness

verses.text: And they were all astonished at the greatness of God. While everyone was marveling at all that Jesus was doing, He said to His disciples,
“Let these words sink into your ears: The Son of Man is about to be delivered into the hands of men.”
But they did not understand this statement. It was veiled from them so that they could not comprehend it, and they were afraid to ask Him about it.
Then an argument started among the disciples as to which of them would be the greatest.
But Jesus, knowing the thoughts of their hearts, had a little child stand beside Him.
And He said to them, “Whoever welcomes this little child in My name welcomes Me, and whoever welcomes Me welcomes the One who sent Me. For whoever is the least among all of you, he is the greatest.”
“Master,” said John, “we saw someone driving out demons in Your name, and we tried to stop him, because he does not accompany us.”
“Do not stop him,” Jesus replied, “for whoever is not against you is for you.”

units.label: He set His face for Jerusalem
units.note: the great hinge of the Gospel

verses.text: As the day of His ascension approached, Jesus resolutely set out for Jerusalem.
He sent messengers on ahead, who went into a village of the Samaritans to make arrangements for Him.
But the people there refused to welcome Him, because He was heading for Jerusalem.
When the disciples James and John saw this, they asked, “Lord, do You want us to call down fire from heaven to consume them?”
But Jesus turned and rebuked them.
And He and His disciples went on to another village.

units.label: The cost of following
units.note: no one who looks back is fit

verses.text: As they were walking along the road, someone said to Jesus, “I will follow You wherever You go.”
Jesus replied, “Foxes have dens and birds of the air have nests, but the Son of Man has no place to lay His head.”
Then He said to another man, “{{akoloutheo|Follow}} Me.” The man replied, “Lord, first let me go and bury my father.”
But Jesus told him, “Let the dead bury their own dead. You, however, go and proclaim the kingdom of God.”
Still another said, “I will follow You, Lord; but first let me bid farewell to my family.”
Then Jesus declared, “No one who puts his hand to the plow and then looks back is fit for the kingdom of God.”

exodos.gloss: A going out, a departure, an exodus. Luke alone records that Moses and Elijah spoke of Jesus' exodos, His departure to be accomplished at Jerusalem. The word ties His death, resurrection, and ascension to the great exodus out of Egypt: Jesus will lead a new rescue through His own death.

doxa.gloss: Glory, splendor, the radiant weight of God's presence. On the mountain the veil is lifted and the disciples glimpse the doxa that belongs to the Son, a foretaste of the glory into which He will enter through the cross and beyond it.

eklegomai.gloss: To choose, to select. The Father names Jesus My Son, whom I have chosen, echoing the Servant of Isaiah 42:1 whom God upholds and delights in. The One chosen and beloved is the One we are commanded to heed above Moses and Elijah.

akoloutheo.gloss: To follow, to accompany as a disciple, to walk the same road. Jesus' call, Follow Me, is a summons to travel His way to Jerusalem, and the closing sayings show that this following allows no rival first claim and no looking back.

basic.context: Jesus takes Peter, John, and James up a mountain to pray, and there His face and clothing blaze with glory. Moses and Elijah appear and speak of His departure, His coming death at Jerusalem. The Father's voice declares Him the chosen Son and commands, Listen to Him. Coming down, the disciples cannot drive a demon out of a boy, they argue about who is greatest, and they fail to grasp Jesus' plain word that He will be handed over. Then at verse 51 everything turns: Jesus sets His face for Jerusalem, and the road to the cross begins. Three would-be followers meet Him on the way, and He tells each that the kingdom asks everything.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the mountain and the road together. The glory of verses 28 to 36 is not detached from the cross; Moses and Elijah speak precisely of His departure, and the same Son revealed in glory is the Son who walks to Jerusalem in verse 51. The travel narrative that opens here governs the next ten chapters of Luke, so this passage is a doorway, not a detour.
basic.originalLanguages: Three Greek words anchor the scene. Moses and Elijah speak of Jesus' exodos, His departure, tying His death to the exodus. The disciples see His doxa, His glory. The Father names Him the Son whom He has chosen, eklegomai, the delighted-in Servant. And the road ends with a call to akoloutheo, to follow, with nothing held back.
basic.typology: Moses the lawgiver and Elijah the prophet meet Jesus and then fade, leaving only the Son; the law and the prophets point to Him and give way to Him. His exodos fulfills the pattern of the first exodus, a greater rescue won not from Egypt but from sin and death.
basic.christ: Jesus is the transfigured, chosen Son of the Father, greater than Moses and Elijah, the One we must heed. Yet His glory is bound to His suffering: He speaks of being delivered into the hands of men, and He sets His face for Jerusalem to accomplish His departure there. The mountain gives a glimpse of the glory the cross will open.
basic.god: The Father Himself speaks from the cloud, identifying His chosen Son and commanding that we listen to Him. God is the one who unveils glory, who ordains the departure at Jerusalem, and whose greatness astonishes the crowd even as His Son walks toward the cross.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Peter 1 recalls the majesty on the holy mountain and the voice from heaven. Isaiah 42 supplies the chosen Servant. Malachi 4 promises Elijah. Philippians 2 traces the same path from self-emptying obedience to exaltation.
basic.oneStory: The chosen Son is unveiled in glory, then turns His face toward Jerusalem to accomplish the departure that rescues His people. Following Him means walking that same road with undivided hearts, for the way to glory runs through the cross.

context.literary: The transfiguration caps the first great phase of Luke's Gospel and confirms Peter's confession. Verse 51 is the hinge of the whole book, opening the long travel narrative that runs to chapter 19 as Jesus journeys toward Jerusalem. The cost-of-discipleship sayings that close the chapter set the tone for the journey.
context.historical: Luke writes for readers who know that Jesus was executed in Jerusalem. The glory on the mountain, the Father's voice, and the deliberate resolve to go to the capital frame that death not as tragedy but as a chosen accomplishment. Samaritan hostility to Jewish pilgrims heading for Jerusalem lies behind the rejection in the village.
context.canonical: The transfiguration echoes Sinai, where God's glory settled in a cloud and His voice was heard, and it anticipates the glory of the risen and ascended Christ. The exodos motif binds Luke's Gospel to the story of Israel's redemption and to its fulfillment in Christ's death, resurrection, and ascension.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal the glory and divine sonship of Jesus, to bind that glory to His coming death, and to mark the decisive turn as He sets out for Jerusalem, calling disciples to a single-minded following that counts the full cost.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The transfiguration and the journey describe unique events in Jesus' mission. The abiding call is prescriptive: listen to the Son, follow Him without rival loyalties, and do not look back once the hand is on the plow.

debates.question: What did the Father mean by naming Jesus the Son whom He has chosen?

views.label: An echo of the chosen Servant
views.case: The wording recalls Isaiah 42:1, My chosen one in whom My soul delights, casting Jesus as the Servant who will suffer to save; this fits the talk of His departure at Jerusalem.

views.label: A declaration of unique divine sonship
views.case: Set beside the fading Moses and Elijah, the words single Jesus out as the beloved and only Son to be heeded above the law and the prophets, in continuity with His baptism.

debates.bearing: Both readings converge: the chosen, beloved Son is also the suffering Servant, so His glory and His cross are held together, and He alone is to be obeyed.

study.originalLanguages: Luke uniquely records that Moses and Elijah spoke of Jesus' exodos, His departure to be accomplished at Jerusalem, framing His death as a new and greater exodus. The disciples glimpse His doxa, the glory veiled in His humanity. The Father names Him the Son whom He has chosen, eklegomai, the language of Isaiah's delighted-in Servant, and commands that we listen to Him. The chapter then turns on the call to akoloutheo, to follow, a following that will allow no competing first claim and no backward look once the plow is grasped.

typology.type: Moses and Elijah yielding to the Son
typology.fulfillment: The law and the prophets bear witness to Jesus and give way to Him, so that only the Son remains to be heard, Hebrews 1:1-2.
Jesus' death, resurrection, and ascension as the true and greater exodus that rescues His people from sin and death, 1 Corinthians 5:7.

typology.type: Glory on the mountain in the cloud
typology.fulfillment: The Sinai glory now resting on the Son, a foretaste of the glory of the risen and ascended Christ, 2 Peter 1:16-18.

study.christ: Jesus stands at the center as the transfigured, chosen Son, greater than Moses and Elijah, the One the Father commands us to heed. Yet Luke will not let His glory float free of His suffering: on the very mountain of light the conversation is about His departure, His death at Jerusalem, and within days He tells the disciples plainly that He will be delivered into the hands of men. At verse 51 He turns His face toward the city where that departure will be accomplished. The glimpse of glory is real, but the road to it runs through the cross, and He walks it willingly for us.
study.god: The Father is the chief actor on the mountain, speaking from the cloud to name and vindicate His chosen Son and to command our listening obedience. It is God's glory that shines, God's purpose that ordains the departure at Jerusalem, and the greatness of God that astonishes the crowd when the boy is healed. The whole scene reveals a God who unveils His Son in splendor and yet leads Him, and all who follow Him, along the way of the cross to true glory.

crossReferences.note: An eyewitness recalls the Majestic Glory and the voice on the holy mountain, This is My beloved Son.
My chosen Servant in whom My soul delights, the background to the Father's naming of Jesus.
The pattern of obedient self-emptying leading to exaltation, mirrored in the transfigured Son who walks to the cross.
Elijah at the mountain of God, now standing with Jesus, the prophet who yields to the Son.

study.oneStory: The Father unveils the glory of His chosen Son, then that same Son sets His face toward Jerusalem to accomplish His exodos, the death and rising that rescue His people. The law and the prophets, Moses and Elijah, give way to Him. To follow this Christ is to take the road He takes, with undivided devotion and no looking back, trusting that the way of the cross opens into the glory glimpsed on the mountain.

quiz.q: What did Moses and Elijah discuss with Jesus on the mountain?

options.text: His departure, His coming death, which He was about to accomplish at Jerusalem.
options.note: Luke 9:31 states they spoke of His exodos, His departure at Jerusalem.

options.text: The glory that Jesus would receive after His suffering.
options.note: Glory is truly in view, but the text specifies they spoke of His departure at Jerusalem.

options.text: How to build the three shelters Peter suggested.
options.note: Peter's shelters were his confused idea; the conversation was about His departure, not the shelters.

options.text: Plans to overthrow Rome by force.
options.note: Nothing of the sort; they spoke of His death, not political revolt.

quiz.q: What did the Father's voice from the cloud command?

options.text: This is My Son, whom I have chosen; listen to Him.
options.note: Luke 9:35 gives the Father's words identifying and commanding obedience to the Son.

options.text: To honor Moses and Elijah alongside Jesus.
options.note: Moses and Elijah appear, but the point is that only the Son remains and must be heard, not that all three are equal.

options.text: To keep the vision secret forever.
options.note: The disciples did keep it to themselves then, but the Father's command was to listen to the Son, not a rule of permanent secrecy.

options.text: To build a temple on the mountain.
options.note: No such command was given; the voice pointed to the Son.

quiz.q: What is the significance of Luke 9:51?

options.text: Jesus resolutely set out for Jerusalem, the great hinge where the journey to the cross begins.
options.note: Verse 51 opens Luke's long travel narrative toward Jerusalem and the cross.

options.text: Jesus decided to visit Jerusalem for a feast like any pilgrim.
options.note: He did go up to Jerusalem, but the verse marks a deliberate, cross-bound resolve, not an ordinary pilgrimage.

options.text: Jesus went to Jerusalem to be crowned an earthly king.
options.note: He goes toward a throne, but by way of the cross, not an earthly coronation.

options.text: Jesus gave up His mission and turned back.
options.note: The opposite: He set His face firmly toward Jerusalem.

quiz.q: What does Jesus teach about following Him at the end of the chapter?

options.text: Following Him allows no rival first claim and no looking back once the hand is on the plow.
options.note: Luke 9:57-62 shows the single-minded cost of following Jesus.

options.text: Following Him is costly and may leave a disciple without earthly comforts.
options.note: True of verse 58, but the fuller point across the three sayings is undivided, unlooking-back devotion.

options.text: Following Him means first settling all family duties before starting.
options.note: Jesus actually refuses to let even a funeral or farewell take first place over the kingdom.

options.text: Following Him requires nothing and costs nothing.
options.note: Contrary to the passage, which stresses the full cost of discipleship.

quiz.q: How does the transfiguration relate to the cross in Luke's telling?

options.text: The glory and the cross are bound together; the chosen Son revealed in glory is the One who walks to Jerusalem to die.
options.note: The glory scene itself speaks of His departure, and verse 51 sends Him to the cross.

options.text: The transfiguration proves Jesus is glorious, and the cross is a later, separate event.
options.note: Both are true events, but Luke deliberately ties them together rather than separating them.

options.text: The transfiguration cancels the need for the cross.
options.note: The glory does not replace the cross; it frames His death as a chosen accomplishment.

options.text: The transfiguration shows Jesus avoiding suffering.
options.note: He does the opposite, setting out for the very city where He will suffer.

questions: The Father's command was simple: listen to Him. In a noisy week, what would it look like to actually give the Son the last word over Moses, Elijah, and every other voice?
Jesus came down from glory straight into a crowd, a failed exorcism, and a squabble about greatness. How does His willingness to enter our mess encourage you today?
At verse 51 Jesus set His face for Jerusalem, knowing what waited there. Where is God asking you for that kind of steady resolve rather than a wavering start?
To one man Jesus said follow Me, and named a good thing, a burial, that could not come first. What good thing are you tempted to let take first place over Him?
No one who looks back is fit for the kingdom. What are you tempted to keep glancing back toward, and what would undivided following look like instead?

translationNotes.note: Departure translates the Greek exodos. The word deliberately recalls Israel's exodus, presenting Jesus' death, resurrection, and ascension as the true and greater rescue accomplished at Jerusalem.

Whom I have chosen follows the earliest manuscripts; a number of later manuscripts read My beloved Son, harmonizing with Matthew 17:5. The BSB footnote records the variant. Either way the Father identifies and vindicates His Son.

Some later manuscripts add that James and John wished to call fire just as Elijah did, and that Jesus said they did not know what spirit they were of, and that the Son of Man came to save. The BSB places these in footnotes as they are absent from the earliest witnesses.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 9:28-62. Headings, line breaks, footnote markers, and poem markers were skipped when building verse text; em and en dashes were converted to commas or clean breaks. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon on Luke 9:28-62 from the trusted cluster was verified this session, the transfiguration messages found were on Mark 9.
